title: Eunomius supersedes Eleusius the Macedonian in the See of Cyzicus, His Origin and Imitation of Aëtius, whose Amanuensis he had been.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The bishop of Constantinople being informed of these circumstances, constituted Eunomius bishop of Cyzicus, inasmuch as he was a person able by his eloquence to win over the minds of the multitude to his own way of thinking.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On his arrival at Cyzicus an imperial edict was published in which it was ordered that Eleusius should be ejected, and Eunomius installed in his place.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This being carried into effect, those who attached themselves to Eleusius, after erecting a sacred edifice without the city, assembled there with him.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But enough has been said of Eleusius: let us now give some account of Eunomius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He had been secretary to Aëtius, surnamed Atheus, of whom we have before spoken, and had learnt from conversing with him, to imitate his sophistical mode of reasoning; being little aware that while exercising himself in framing fallacious arguments, and in the use of certain insignificant terms, he was really deceiving himself.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This habit however inflated him with pride, and he fell into blasphemous heresies, and so became an advocate of the dogmas of Arius, and in various ways an adversary to the doctrines of truth.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And as he had but a very slender knowledge of the letter of Scripture, he was wholly unable to enter into the spirit of it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Yet he abounded in words, and was accustomed to repeat the same thoughts in different terms, without ever arriving at a clear explanation of what he had proposed to himself.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of this his seven books On the Apostle’s Epistle to the Romans, on which he bestowed a quantity of vain labor, is a remarkable proof: for although he has employed an immense number of words in the attempt to expound it, he has by no means succeeded in apprehending the scope and object of that epistle.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: All other works of his extant are of a similar character, in which he that would take the trouble to examine them, would find a great scarcity of sense, amidst a profusion of verbiage.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This Eunomius Eudoxius promoted to the see of Cyzicus; who being come thither, astonished his auditors by the extraordinary display of his ‘dialectic’ art, and thus a great sensation was produced at Cyzicus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At length the people unable to endure any longer the empty and assumptions parade of his language, drove him out of their city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He therefore withdrew to Constantinople, and taking up his abode with Eudoxius, was regarded as a titular bishop.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But lest we should seem to have said these things for the sake of detraction, let us hear what Eunomius himself has the hardihood to utter in his sophistical discourses concerning the Deity himself, for he uses the following language: ‘God knows no more of his own substance than we do; nor is this more known to him, and less to us: but whatever we know about the Divine substance, that precisely is known to God; and on the other hand, whatever he knows, the same also you will find without any difference in us.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This and many other similar tedious and absurd fallacies Eunomius was accustomed to draw up in utter insensibility to his own folly.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On what account he afterwards separated from the Arians, we shall state in its proper place.
